coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from discarded cigarette butts as an effective fluorescence probe for sensing iron ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Min
Yan, Yujia
He, Fengqin
Wang, Zijian
Liu, Enzhou
Fan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: A green synthesis method for preparing fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from discarded cigarette butts was described. This method reduces costs and environmental pollution and is fully in line with the development of green chemistry. The as-synthesized CDs can be designed as a fluorescent probe for detecting iron ions (Fe3+) with highly selective and sensitive detection in the range of 0.5–800 μM. The reliability of the proposed probe was studied using tap water as a real sample, and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cigarette butts
Carbon materials
Sensors
Iron ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM images (b) of CDs sample.
UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs (a), and fluorescence intensity values of CDs under different irradiation times (b).
Fluorescence spectra of CDs with various concentrations of Fe3+ (a), the linear relationship (b) and the interference experiments (c and d).
Detection results of Fe3+ in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from discarded cigarette butts as an effective fluorescence probe for sensing iron ions

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological and Environmental Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A green synthesis method for preparing fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) from discarded cigarette butts was described. This method reduces costs and environmental pollution and is fully in line with the development of green chemistry. The as-synthesized CDs can be designed as a fluorescent probe for detecting iron ions (Fe3+) with highly selective and sensitive detection in the range of 0.5–800 μM. The reliability of the proposed probe was studied using tap water as a real sample, and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an essential transition metal element, Fe3+ has important physiological functions for almost all organisms, as it can easily give and receive electrons without striking a blow [1]. Meanwhile, the concentration level of Fe3+ can serve as an indicator for evaluating water quality due to its potential to cause discomfort among consumers. The World Health Organization (WHO) stipulates that its content should not exceed 0.3 mg/L [2]. Therefore, it is an urgent need to design a novel sensor for sensing Fe3+.

Currently, the main analytical methods for the detection of Fe3+ are inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, electrochemistry, atomic absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence (FL) spectroscopy [3,4]. Among these analytical techniques, fluorescence analysis has attracted the interest of the majority of researchers due to its rapidity and simplicity [5]. Meanwhile, carbon dots (CDs) as a new-fashioned zero-dimensional photoluminescent material have been widely used in the design of fluorescent probes due to the advantages of uniform size distribution, photochemical stability, low toxicity and desired biocompatibility [6]. Particularly, the utilization of eco-friendly, low-cost and inexhaustible carbonaceous biomass as carbon sources for the synthesis of CDs has a promising future, fully meeting green chemistry development requirements [7].

In this study, a green and economical route for the preparation of CDs from waste cigarette butts by a one-step hydrothermal method is described. The sensitivity and selectivity detection of Fe3+ was achieved based on the formation of non-fluorescent complexes between CDs and Fe3+. Moreover, the developed fluorescent probe effectively was used to detect Fe3+ concentration in tap water with satisfactory results.
Experimental materials and apparatus are supplied in Sup. porting Information.

1.0 g cleaned dry cigarette butt fibers were fully immersed in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and then transferred to an autoclave for reacting at 200 °C for 6 h. The unreacted impurities and large particles were then removed by vacuum filtering and centrifuging at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, respectively. Finally, the collected supernatant was further filtered through 0.22 μm water filter and then dialyzed against 150 mL of ultrapure water using a dialysis bag (MW = 2000) for 12 h. The purified CDs were stored at 4 °C for future use.

2.0 mL of freshly prepared Fe3+ solutions at different concentrations (0–800 μM) were incubated with 2 mL of purified CDs for 1 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of all samples were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm and the F0/F ratio was calculated as the detection signals for sensing Fe3+. And, the designed CDs-based fluorescent probes were used to detect the concentration levels of Fe3+ in tap water samples based on the standard addition method.

The synthesized CDs were uniformly dispersed and approximately spherical particles with an average particle size of 3.8 nm (Fig. 1 a). Also, the CDs nanoparticles exhibited an identifiable lattice structure with a lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 1b), corresponding to the (100) lattice plane of graphitic carbon [8]. A broad diffraction peak was observed at 2θ = 21.5° in the XRD pattern (F. S1a), indicating that the resulting CDs have graphitic nature [9]. The FTIR spectrum (F. S1b) indicating that the surface structure of CDs was mainly composed of hydrophilic carboxylic and hydroxyl groups [10]. Furthermore, the XPS analysis (F. S2) revealing that the CDs was mainly comprised of C and O elements [11].

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs has a distinct sharp peak at 280 nm (Fig. 2 a), which originates from the π-π* transition of C-C bond [12]. Additionally, the fluorescence emission center of CDs was located at 435 nm with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (Fig. 2a). As shown in F. S3, the prepared CDs possessed concentration/excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior owing to the presence of different surface states [13]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of the emission center did not decline significantly with the prolongation of radiation time, indicating the prepared CDs had good resistance to photobleaching resistance (Fig. 2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as fluorescent probe for sensing Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: The optimization of the assay conditions showed that a maximum response was achieved by incubating 2 mL CDs with Fe3+ for 1 min in a neutral environment (F. S4a – d). Fig. 3 a revealed the fluorescence emission of CDs gradually quenching with the increase of Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.5–800 μM. The fluorescence quenching behavior followed the Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F-1 = KSV (Fig. 3b), where KSV corresponded to the quenching constant of 0.0026 μM−1, F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 435 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The detection limit value was calculated as 0.2 μM based on based on 3σ/k criteria, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement and k is the slope of the standard curve. the fluorescence signal of CDs did not show significant changes.

However, the fluorescence performance of CDs did not show significant changes compared to other metal ions at the concentration of 500 μM (Fig. 3c and F. S5a). In addition, the existence of other metal ions had a negligible impact on the detection signal (Fig. 3d), showing that CDs have strong specificity for Fe3+.

Finally, the sensing mechanism of the system was explored, and it can be ruled out that CDs aggregation causes fluorescence quenching due to the fluorescence emission center of CDs does not red-shift when Fe3+ is present [14] (Fig. 3a). However, a new absorption peak appeared at 356 nm and the absorption peak of CDs around 280 nm disappeared after addition of Fe3+ (F. S5b), and the average fluorescence lifetimes of CDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were 1.034 and 1.048 ns, respectively, (F. S6). This powerfully proved that the formation of a coordinating complex between the CDs and Fe3+ leads to the fluorescence quenching of CDs [15].

To research the reliability of CDs in sensing Fe3+, the constructed fluorescent probe was used to evaluate the concentration levels of Fe3+ in tap water samples based on the standard addition method with satisfactory results obtained (HYPERLINK "SPS:refid::tbl1" Table

1). Moreover, the detection performance of the proposed method was comparable with other Fe3+ detection techniques reported in literature in T S1. Most importantly, the use of discarded cigarette butts as a carbon source to prepare CDs not only saved cost but also reduced environmental pollution, which perfectly fits the development of green chemistry.

In summary, a new fluorescent probe was designed for the sensitive and selective determination of Fe3+ based on CDs obtained from discarded cigarette butts. And, the designed fluorescent probe was applied to the determination of Fe3+ in the tap water samples with satisfactory results obtained, which further promoted the development of CDs in the fields of molecular detection.

Min Yang: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Yujia Yan: Formal analysis. Fengqin He: Funding acquisition. Zijian Wang: Validation. Enzhou Liu: Writing – review & editing. Jun Fan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22078261 and 32270530), the Project funded by Shaanxi Provincial Department of Education Research Program Project (23JK0631).

L. Li, L.H. Shi, J. Jia, et al., Analyst. 145 (2020) 5450-5457.
